article_title: I ditched browser bookmarks for a single text file & my links have never been more organized
core_thesis: Traditional browser bookmarks become cluttered and unmanageable over time due to lack of structure and context. The author advocates replacing them with a YAML-based, self-hosted system called 'Links as Code' that stores all links in a single plain-text file, offering portability, intentionality, and easy maintenance.
detailed_summary: The article describes the author's frustration with conventional browser bookmarks, which accumulate quickly, become disorganized, and lack context. They discovered a tool called 'Links as Code' that reads a YAML file and renders it as a clean, minimal dashboard. The YAML file contains structured entries (name, URL, optional description) grouped into sections. The tool is deployed via Docker using a simple compose file, with no database or syncing required. The author found that this approach forces deliberate link saving, keeps the list clean, and makes reorganization trivial. They now use it as their primary entry point for all self-hosted services, media tools, and infrastructure, grouping links by usage rather than arbitrary folders. The article emphasizes that the simplicity and control of a text file outweigh the initial friction of manual entry.

entities.name: Links as Code
entities.role: A Docker-based app that turns a YAML file into a structured, self-hosted bookmark dashboard.

entities.name: Dhruv Bhutani
entities.role: Author of the article, technology writer focusing on productivity, self-hosting, and networking.

entities.name: YAML
entities.role: A human-readable data serialization format used to store bookmarks as structured data in a single text file.

key_data_points: Traditional browser bookmarks lack structure and context, leading to clutter and outdated links (🟢 relevant to your productivity: a cleaner system reduces cognitive load and saves time).
Links as Code is deployed via a Docker compose file in seconds, with no database or account needed (🟢 relevant to your homelab/DevOps: easy self-hosted deployment fits your infrastructure).
The YAML file supports ungrouped links and grouped sections, allowing flexible organization based on usage patterns (🟢 relevant to your programming: YAML is familiar and scriptable).

Adding a link requires manually editing the text file, which forces intentional saving and reduces clutter (🟢 relevant to your student productivity: deliberate action improves focus and organization).
The tool is minimal—no automation or recommendations—which the author considers a strength (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: simplicity reduces attack surface and maintenance overhead).
methodology_steps: Deploy Links as Code using the provided Docker compose file: place the YAML file in a data directory and run the container.
Create a YAML file with entries structured as: name, URL, and optional description. Group links into sections (e.g., 'Core Tools', 'Media Services', 'Infrastructure').
Edit the YAML file directly to add, remove, or reorganize links. No browser UI needed.
Access the rendered dashboard via the app's web interface, which displays the links as a clean homepage.
conclusions: A YAML-based bookmark system offers superior organization and maintainability compared to traditional browser bookmarks, as it forces intentional saving and easy restructuring.
The simplicity of a single text file eliminates syncing issues and vendor lock-in, making it ideal for self-hosted environments.
The initial friction of manual entry is offset by long-term cleanliness and control, aligning with minimalist productivity philosophies.
personal_relevance_summary: This article directly addresses your need for better organization and productivity as a student and programmer. The YAML-based approach is scriptable and fits into your existing homelab/DevOps workflow (Docker deployment). For Red Teaming, you could adapt this to manage C2 infrastructure links, exploit databases, or research resources in a clean, portable format. The deliberate saving habit also reduces digital clutter, freeing mental bandwidth for more important tasks.
extra_advice: Consider extending this concept by writing a script that automatically adds bookmarks from your browser or a shared clipboard to the YAML file, combining the intentionality of manual entry with some automation. You could also version-control the YAML file with Git to track changes and collaborate with a team.
